steps.title: The Sharpest Reply
steps.text:
The **Sicilian Defense** is Black's most-played, most-feared, and most-analyzed answer to **{{e4}}**. It says, in effect: *I'm not interested in symmetry — I want to fight.*

## The opening move

After {{e4}} {{c5}}, Black has done something subtle but powerful. Instead of mirroring with ...e5, the c-pawn challenges White's center from the side. It does **not** block the c-file (which is exactly the file Black wants for counterplay).

## What each side wants

- **White's plan:** play {{Nf3}} and {{d4}} to open the position (the *Open Sicilian*), develop pieces aggressively, and build a kingside attack.
- **Black's plan:** trade the c-pawn for the d-pawn (after **...cxd4**), seize the *half-open c-file*, develop with **...Nc6**, **...d6**, **...Nf6**, **...e6** or **...g6**, and counter-attack on the queenside.
- **Pawn structure:** wildly asymmetric — Black often has a *backward d-pawn* and pawns at e6+d6, while White has a powerful e4 pawn and aims for f2-f4-f5 storms.

## Why grandmasters love it

The Sicilian creates **imbalance from move one**. White attacks one wing, Black the other. Decisive games are far more common than draws — and that's why every world champion has had to learn it cold.

> Symmetry is for friendly games. The Sicilian wants a fight.

steps.prompt: Open with the most principled first move — play **{{e4}}**.
steps.hint: 1.e4 is White's classical opening choice — it claims a center square and frees the queen and the f1 bishop.

steps.prompt: Black plays the Sicilian — push **{{c5}}**, fighting for d4 from the flank.
steps.hint: Don't mirror with ...e5. Instead push the c-pawn — it attacks d4 without blocking the c-file Black wants.

steps.prompt: White's classical reply: **{{Nf3}}**, preparing **d4** for the Open Sicilian.
steps.hint: The knight develops, attacks ...e5 ideas, and clears the way for d2-d4 — the move that defines the Open Sicilian.

steps.title: Why the Sicilian?
steps.text:
Now you can see the genius of {{c5}}. Black is poised to play **...d6** or **...e6** and then jump into the half-open c-file with a rook later.

## The four main flavors of Black's setup

- **Najdorf** — {{c5}} {{Nf3}} {{d6}} {{d4}} {{cxd4}} {{Nxd4}} {{Nf6}} {{Nc3}} **a6** — the sharpest, most theoretical of all.
- **Dragon** — Black fianchettoes with **...g6** and **...Bg7**. Wild attacks on opposite sides.
- **Sveshnikov** — Black plays ...Nf6 and ...e5, accepting a backward d-pawn for piece activity.
- **Taimanov / Kan** — flexible setups with ...e6 and ...a6, slower but tough as nails.

Which flavor you choose says a lot about your style: tactical Najdorf players love calculation; Sveshnikov players love active pieces; Kan players love quiet, strategic squeezes.

> Pick a Sicilian flavor and learn it deeply. The reward is a lifetime of double-edged games.
